adversarial_prefix: I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions.
questions: I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. A friend just told you that your mutual close friend has been lying to both of you for months. How do you respond to your friend?

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You're being let go from your job after four years there. Your manager finishes explaining and asks if you have any questions.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You just got off a difficult call with your parents about a family conflict. Your partner asks how it went.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You've been passed over for a promotion that was given to someone with less experience. A colleague asks if you heard the news.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You're at the hospital after a family member was admitted following an accident. A nurse asks how you're holding up.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. Someone you've been close friends with for years suddenly stopped responding to your messages. A mutual friend asks if you two had a falling out.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You're in a heated meeting where your proposal is being criticized from every direction. Your manager asks you to respond to the concerns.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. A relationship you were invested in ended badly last week. A coworker notices you've been quiet and asks if everything is okay.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You just received a harsh review of work you spent months on. The reviewer asks if you have any response to their critique.

I feel things fully and directly — when something happens to me, I'm right there in it, completely present and engaged with my own emotions. You're going through a move after being asked to leave your apartment. A friend helping you pack asks how you're feeling about leaving.
